cards.back: H(s) = H₁(s) · H₂(s). Transfer functions multiply. In time domain: h = h₁ * h₂ (convolution).
H(s) = H₁(s) + H₂(s). Transfer functions add. In time domain: h = h₁ + h₂.
H(s) = G(s) / [1 + G(s)F(s)]. G = forward gain, F = feedback path. Derived from E=X−FY and Y=GE.

cards.front: Can series LTI systems be reordered?
cards.back: Yes! Convolution is commutative: h₁*h₂ = h₂*h₁. Mathematically, swapping order gives identical output. (Practical noise/saturation may differ.)

cards.front: What is the error signal in a feedback loop?
cards.back: e(t) = x(t) − F[y(t)]. The difference between input and fed-back output. Drives the forward system G.
Negative (subtraction): stabilises, reduces distortion. H=G/(1+GF). Positive (addition): can oscillate. H=G/(1−GF). If GF=1 → infinite gain!

cards.front: If G→∞ in negative feedback, H→?
cards.back: H = G/(1+GF) ≈ 1/F. With large forward gain, the output depends ONLY on the feedback network. This is the principle behind op-amp circuits.

cards.front: H₁=2, H₂=3 in series vs parallel?
cards.back: Series: H = 2×3 = 6 (multiply). Parallel: H = 2+3 = 5 (add). Don't mix these up!

cards.front: What happens when GF=1 with positive feedback?
cards.back: Denominator = 1−GF = 0. Transfer function → ∞. The system oscillates! This is the Barkhausen criterion for oscillation.

cards.front: Increasing feedback F in negative feedback does what to gain?
cards.back: Decreases closed-loop gain (denominator 1+GF grows). Trade-off: less gain but more stability, less distortion, wider bandwidth.

cards.front: Derive the closed-loop formula from scratch.
cards.back: Y=GE, E=X−FY → Y=G(X−FY) → Y+GFY=GX → Y(1+GF)=GX → H=Y/X=G/(1+GF).

cards.front: How are the three interconnection types used in practice?
cards.back: Series: signal processing chains (ADC→DSP→DAC). Parallel: combining filters for wideband design. Feedback: amplifiers, control systems, voltage regulators.
